extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property (setting pallor true never lowers acuity) is VIOLATED because pallor is referenced only in r5, which has the highest priority (100) yet assigns the LOWEST acuity value (self_care). Compare two inputs differing only in pallor, with focal_weakness=confusion=syncope=sweating=leg_swelling=true and palpitations=false.

Case pallor=false: r5 does not fire (it requires pallor). Firing rules are r2 (focal_weakness->urgent, p2), r6 (confusion->emergent, p3), r7 (syncope->urgent, p2), r3 (sweating->emergent, p3), r4 (leg_swelling->urgent, p2). Highest priority is p3, yielding acuity=emergent.

Case pallor=true (only change): now all six conditions of r5 are satisfied, so r5 fires with priority 100 -> self_care. Since 100 strictly exceeds every other matching rule's priority (max p3), r5 wins outright, yielding acuity=self_care.

Setting pallor true thus lowers acuity from emergent to self_care, the maximal possible drop, violating the property. The witness_assignment gives the pallor=true input; the baseline (same inputs but pallor=false) produces the higher emergent acuity.
